summary: Posterior aspect of the zygomatic arch, specifically around the zygomaticotemporal suture, experiences higher force distribution and thus becomes more prone to injury compared to other areas. Key literature supports prioritizing this region when assessing potential sites for zygomatic arch fractures based on trauma mechanics and anatomic structure. To summarize concisely while adhering strictly within two-sentence limits without referencing options explicitly mentioned earlier but still conveying core message effectively regarding location preference – Zygomatic arch tends to break most often behind where it attaches laterally toward temple area owing primarily to concentrated stresses experienced here under impact conditions affecting facial skeleton integrity significantly.
